Priya instead runs the four-surface check before publishing.

**On-page checklist (45 minutes).**
- Title tag: 'Employee Expense Reimbursement Policy Template (Free, 2026)' — 62 chars, close to the limit but scannable, target query up front.
- URL: `/guides/expense-reimbursement-policy-template` — short, keyword-bearing.
- H1: 'Employee Expense Reimbursement Policy Template' — single, direct.
- H2 structure: 'What a reimbursement policy should cover' / 'IRS rules at a glance' / 'Sample policy language you can copy' / 'Common mistakes' / 'Download the template' — each section a sub-query searchers would actually ask.
- Meta description: 'A free, customizable employee expense reimbursement policy template — plus IRS rules, sample language, and the common mistakes to avoid.' — 142 chars, no keyword stuffing.
- Internal links: Priya links into the new article from three existing pages — her 'expense report automation' post, her 'per diem rates' post, and her pricing page's FAQ. She also adds two internal links *out* of the new article to related internal content.
- Schema: she adds Article schema plus HowTo schema for the 'How to write your policy in six steps' section.
- Images: every image has descriptive alt text ('Sample expense reimbursement policy section for travel per diems' rather than 'image1.png').
- Keyword density: she resists the urge to repeat the target query awkwardly. It appears in the title, H1, URL, first paragraph, one H2, and naturally in the body — maybe 9 times in 2,800 words. That's fine.

**Technical surface (one-time, already done).** Her site is HTTPS, mobile-responsive, LCP ~1.9s, CLS 0.04, XML sitemap in Search Console. She pings Google to recrawl the new URL via Search Console.

**Link-building (first 90 days).** She identifies 12 realistic link targets:
- Four small accounting/bookkeeping blogs that cover policy-template topics — cold pitch with the downloadable template as the hook.
- Two SHRM-style HR publications — pitch an exclusive 'what we learned from analyzing 500 reimbursement policies' piece that cites her template.
- One Reddit answer on r/smallbusiness about reimbursement policies that she writes (genuinely, not spammy) with a link to the template as one of several references.
- Five existing customer companies she asks to link to the template from their internal documentation or public resources page.
- One LinkedIn article she writes that links back to the template.

Realistic expected yield over 90 days: 3–5 new referring domains for the page. Small — but for a new page, directionally useful and compounding with the article's internal links.

**Monitoring (weekly, 5 min).** Priya opens Search Console every Monday, checks average position and impressions for the target query. At week 3, position 42. Week 6, position 23. Week 10, position 14. Week 14, position 9. At page 1, click-through rate jumps an order of magnitude and traffic becomes meaningful. She writes two more articles that internally link back to this one, which pushes it to position 6 by week 20.

**The compounding payoff.** By month 8, the page is position 4, producing ~85 visits/month and converting ~1 customer/month. That continues. By month 18, three newer articles in the same cluster have raised the domain's topical authority and lifted the original page to position 2, producing ~220 visits/month and ~2.5 customers/month. All the on-page and technical work was done once. All the links were earned in the first 90 days. The customer flow compounds for years.

**What the cofounder's 'just publish' version would have missed.** No internal links means Google takes 3–4× longer to discover and trust the page. No outbound pitching means no new referring domains and the authority pillar stays at zero. No schema means no rich snippet lift in CTR. No URL slug optimization means the page starts with a fraction of the ranking signal. Collectively, 'just publish' typically adds 4–9 months to the timeline to rank — and often means the page never reaches page 1 at all.

contrast.explanation: The how-Google-ranks concept names the three pillars (intent, depth, authority). This concept is the tactical execution that actually produces those pillars. On-page and technical work make sure Google can *see* the match with the first two pillars — but good on-page work on a page that already had good intent and depth is additive, not transformative. Link-building is the one tactical surface that directly moves the authority pillar, and it's the surface founder-run programs most consistently skip. Getting the three pillars *right* in concept without doing the tactical work to express them is a common failure mode.

flashcards.front: Name the four tactical surfaces of SEO work and which one is ongoing vs. one-time.
flashcards.back: (1) Keyword research — upfront per topic. (2) On-page optimization — one-time per page, roughly a 60-minute checklist. (3) Link-building — ongoing; the pillar that compounds. (4) Technical SEO — mostly a one-time audit plus monitoring. Link-building is the ongoing one and the one founder-run programs most often under-invest in.

flashcards.front: List at least five items on the per-page on-page SEO checklist.
flashcards.back: Any five of: title tag with target keyword (50–60 chars); single clear H1; short keyword-bearing URL slug; strong meta description; logical H2/H3 heading structure; internal links in from related pages and out to related pages; schema markup (Article, FAQ, HowTo); descriptive alt text on images; natural keyword placement (no stuffing).

flashcards.front: The reliable link quality hierarchy from best to worst is: ______ > pitched > widget > ______. The last category is against Google's guidelines and will eventually trigger ranking penalties.
flashcards.back: earned ; bought — earned links from credible sources are the core; bought links are the dominant 'black-hat' mistake and the textbook way to produce a short-term lift followed by a ranking collapse.

flashcards.front: Which of these is the most common under-investment in founder-run SEO programs? (a) Keyword research, (b) On-page optimization, (c) Link-building, (d) Technical SEO.
flashcards.back: (c) Link-building. On-page and technical are checklists that feel like work and get done. Keyword research is also typically fine. Link-building requires outreach, relationship work, content pitching, and PR — it's uncomfortable, slow, and ongoing, and it's the surface that most consistently gets skipped. It's also the pillar that most directly determines whether a page can beat competitors with authority gaps.

flashcards.front: Name at least three technical SEO checks that, if broken, will silently kill rankings across an otherwise well-optimized site.
flashcards.back: Any three of: robots.txt accidentally blocking important pages; incorrect or missing canonical tags causing duplicate-content dilution; broken mobile rendering (Google indexes mobile-first); slow page speed / poor Core Web Vitals (especially LCP > 2.5s); missing or malformed XML sitemap; noindex meta tags left on production pages; HTTPS misconfiguration. These are 'silent' because the content and on-page can look perfect while rankings quietly never materialize.

scenarios.setup: A startup has published 60 articles over 6 months. On-page is clean — titles, H1s, URLs, internal linking all look right. Technical audit shows no issues. Search Console shows articles indexed but ranking in positions 30–70 on all target queries, not moving up over 4 months of monitoring.
scenarios.question: Which pillar are they most likely missing, and what's the fix?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Authority — specifically, inbound links from credible external sites. The symptoms are classic: on-page and technical are both fine (so Google can see and understand the pages), indexing works (so Google has found them), and articles rank — just in the mid-range where domains without authority land by default. Pages that sit at position 30–70 for months without moving are almost always authority-limited: the content is good enough to exist in Google's index, but there's no external signal telling Google this site's content deserves to be on page 1. Fix: start a systematic link-earning program. (1) Identify the 10–15 articles with the strongest commercial value and focus link-building on those specifically, not spread across all 60. (2) For each, identify 20–30 realistic outreach targets (bloggers, journalists, industry newsletters, related tool makers) and pitch them with a specific hook — original data, a quote, a useful resource. (3) Run a PR push on any launches or data the company has that would give a journalist a reason to cite them. (4) Investigate whether product pages can become naturally link-attracting — profile pages, data pages, free tools. Realistic outcome: 30–60 new referring domains over 6 months would move the focused articles from position 40 into page 1 for most of their target queries. Content and technical are the floor; authority is the lever.
rubric: Correctly diagnoses authority/link-building as the missing pillar
Names the 'stuck at position 30–70' pattern as authority-limited
Prescribes focusing link-building on highest-value articles
Names specific outreach targets (bloggers, journalists, related tools)
Connects link-building back to the three-pillar model

scenarios.setup: A founder has been approached by an SEO agency offering '50 high-authority guest post backlinks per month, DR60+ sites, $3,000/month.' They're willing to sign a 6-month contract and guarantee ranking improvements.
scenarios.question: Evaluate this offer. What's almost certainly going on, and what's the right response?
scenarios.modelAnswer: This is almost certainly a paid-link scheme dressed up as 'guest posts.' Real high-authority DR60+ sites do not accept 50 guest posts per month at this price point from a random SEO agency — the math doesn't work for legitimate publishers. What's actually happening is either (a) a private blog network (PBN) where the agency owns or controls the linking sites, (b) expired-domain sites that rank but provide no real editorial value, or (c) genuine guest-post placements obtained via payment to the publisher — which is also against Google's guidelines even when the publishers are real. All three are 'black-hat' tactics that can work for a quarter or two and then trigger a ranking collapse when Google updates detection, usually wiping out not just the bought links but suppressing the entire domain's organic visibility. Response: don't sign. The 'guaranteed ranking improvements' language is a tell — legitimate SEO can't guarantee specific rankings because Google's algorithm isn't controllable; agencies that guarantee rankings are effectively guaranteeing they'll manipulate signals in ways Google will eventually penalize. Redirect the $3k/month toward: genuine content production, PR outreach, original research the industry will cite, and product-led link attraction. The slower, white-hat approach is the only one that compounds rather than eventually crashing.
rubric: Identifies the offer as almost certainly a paid-link/PBN scheme
Names the short-term-lift-then-crash pattern
Names specific black-hat variants (PBN, paid guest posts, expired domains)
Calls out 'guaranteed rankings' as an illegitimate claim
Redirects to specific legitimate alternatives

scenarios.setup: A team rebuilt their website on a new framework over the weekend. Three weeks later, organic traffic is down 60% and they can't figure out why. On-page content and article URLs look the same to them.
scenarios.question: Walk through the technical-SEO checklist you'd run, in priority order.
scenarios.modelAnswer: This is the classic 'rebuild killed SEO' pattern — technical SEO issues introduced by the rebuild are almost certainly the cause. Priority order: (1) Robots.txt — check whether the new deploy accidentally ships with 'Disallow: /' (common staging-to-production bug). If so, Google is crawling nothing. (2) Meta robots — check whether pages have 'noindex' tags left from staging. Many frameworks noindex by default in dev mode. (3) URL structure — check whether URLs changed even subtly (trailing slashes, parameter order, new slug patterns). If URLs changed, Google sees the entire content library as new; old rankings don't transfer unless 301 redirects point old→new. (4) XML sitemap — check that it exists, is submitted to Search Console, and lists the correct new URLs. (5) Canonical tags — check that they point to correct URLs, not staging/dev URLs. (6) Core Web Vitals — check that the new framework didn't regress page speed; a 3-second LCP regression can demote pages. (7) Mobile rendering — check that the new responsive layout actually works and isn't hiding content on mobile. (8) HTTP status codes — Google Search Console's Coverage report will surface 404s, 5xx errors, and soft 404s. Most likely culprit in this specific case: missing 301 redirects from old URLs to new ones, or a robots.txt disallow that shipped from staging. Fix the highest-priority item first; rankings typically recover within 2–6 weeks once the technical issue is resolved, though some recovery can take longer. General lesson: any site rebuild should include a pre-launch SEO checklist and a post-launch Search Console watch, because the ways a rebuild can silently kill rankings are many and the recovery time is weeks.
rubric: Identifies the rebuild as the likely cause
Prioritizes robots.txt / noindex / URL changes as top suspects
Names 301 redirects as critical for URL changes
Mentions Core Web Vitals / speed regression
Prescribes Search Console Coverage report as a diagnostic
Notes the 2–6 week recovery timeline

teachback.prompt: Teach a developer co-founder the four tactical surfaces of SEO work: keyword research, on-page, link-building, and technical. For each, cover the key actions, which ones are one-time vs. ongoing, and which surface is the most commonly under-invested. Include at least three specific on-page checklist items and three specific technical checks.
rubric: Names all four surfaces correctly
Names at least three on-page items (title, H1, URL, internal links, schema, alt text, meta description)
Names at least three technical checks (crawlability, page speed, mobile, canonical, HTTPS, sitemaps)
Identifies link-building as the ongoing pillar and the most under-invested surface
States the link-quality hierarchy (earned > pitched > widget > bought)
Warns specifically against buying links and the short-lift-then-crash pattern

concepts.title: Testing SEO Cheaply When the Signal Is Slow

pretest.prompt: In SEM you can test a keyword with $200 over two weeks and get a clear signal. In SEO, you can spend $20,000 over two months and see nothing. Before reading on — describe what 'cheap testing' could possibly mean for SEO, given that the channel's signal is inherently slow. What specifically can you test in a week, and what can you only test over 3–6 months?
pretest.modelAnswer: Traditional A/B testing is impossible for SEO — you can't run a two-week test the way you can for SEM. But 'testing' can be decomposed. What you *can* test fast, at near-zero cost: (1) Does search demand exist for your topic? (keyword tools tell you this in an hour). (2) Does the target query convert when you pay for the click? (run a small SEM campaign on the same keyword — a working SEM conversion rate means the organic rank, when it arrives, will also convert). (3) Are there rankable queries in your niche? (competitor analysis and SERP inspection reveal this in a day). What you can only test over months: (4) Can your specific content actually rank? (publish 3–5 ambitious test articles, watch them for 90 days, measure whether they break into page 2, page 1, and top 5 on the expected timeline). The cheap-test move is to front-load everything that can be validated in days, then spend modestly on a 3–5 article test batch — not commit fully — and let the 60–90 day ranking trajectory decide whether to scale the program.

lesson.markdown:
## The SEO testing paradox

In SEM you can settle a keyword's viability in two weeks with $200. In SEO the same question takes 3–6 months and several thousand dollars of content. You cannot A/B test SEO the way you A/B test ads. The feedback loop is too slow. So what does 'testing SEO cheaply' actually mean?

The answer: decompose the question. 'Will SEO work for our business?' is not a single test. It's a chain of four sub-questions, three of which can be answered fast and one of which cannot.

## The four sub-questions

**1. Does search demand exist for our topic? (Answerable in an hour.)** Use Google Keyword Planner (free with any Google Ads account), Ahrefs, or SEMrush to pull search volumes for 30–100 candidate queries. If the combined monthly volume across your plausible long-tail is under ~5,000 searches and there's no meaningful head to climb toward, SEO is not going to be a primary channel no matter how good the articles are. No search demand = no SEO. This test costs an hour and protects you from the most common SEO failure mode: spending six months learning that nobody is typing what you planned to rank for.

**2. Do those queries convert commercially? (Answerable in 2–4 weeks with a small SEM test, typically $300–$1,000.)** This is the chapter's single most underused trick: **run Google Ads on your target SEO keywords first.** If SEM clicks on those keywords convert to customers, then SEO rankings on those same keywords will also convert (they're the same users at the same intent). If SEM clicks don't convert, SEO rankings won't either — no matter how much you spend on content. A small SEM test settles the commercial-intent question months before SEO would. The classic failure mode here is ecommerce stores ranking for 'free [product]' variants and discovering those searchers never buy.

**3. Are there rankable queries in our niche, given our current authority? (Answerable in a day.)** For each candidate query, pull the current top 10 results. Inspect their referring-domain counts (via Ahrefs, Moz, or similar) and content depth. If the top 10 are all multi-thousand-word articles on sites with 500+ referring domains each, you will not rank there inside 18 months regardless of content quality. If several of the top 10 are thin content from low-authority domains, there's a realistic ranking path. This is the Rand Fishkin page-1-feasibility check in tactical form.

**4. Can our specific content actually rank on the timeline we expected? (Answerable only by shipping and waiting 60–90 days.)** This is the irreducibly slow part. You publish 3–5 well-made articles on queries that passed checks 1–3, link to them from existing content, pitch a few external links, and then watch their positions in Search Console over 60–90 days. The trajectory is the signal.

## The cheap-test playbook

For a founder deciding whether to commit $50k+ to an SEO program, the cheap pre-commit sequence looks like this:

1. **Week 1:** Keyword research. Build a list of 50–100 candidate queries with volumes and competition metrics. Cost: ~$50 in tool subscriptions, a few hours of time.
2. **Weeks 1–2:** SERP analysis. For the top 10 candidates, inspect current rankers and authority profiles. Kill queries where the page-1 gap is unbridgeable in 18 months.
3. **Weeks 1–4:** SEM intent test. Put $500–$1,000 into Google Ads on a subset of candidate keywords. Measure click-to-conversion rate on those exact queries. Any query where SEM conversion is near zero gets killed from the SEO plan regardless of volume or competition profile.
4. **Weeks 2–6:** Publish 3–5 test articles on the queries that survived steps 1–3. Each article is ambitious (real depth, well-structured, well-linked internally). Total investment: $4,000–$8,000 depending on content cost.
5. **Weeks 6–20:** Monitor. Check Search Console weekly for: impressions (is Google showing these pages at all?), average position (is it improving?), and indexing (are the pages in the index?). Ship 1–2 external links per article if possible.
6. **Week 13 decision point:** By 90 days, you should see articles landing somewhere on pages 1–3 (not necessarily top 10 yet, but moving). Impressions should be rising, not flat. If 3–4 of the 5 test articles show upward movement, SEO is working for your niche and the program is worth scaling. If all 5 are stuck in positions 40+ with flat impressions, either the authority gap is bigger than you assessed, your content isn't deep enough, or the intent match is wrong — diagnose before scaling.

Total cost of the cheap test: roughly $5,000–$10,000 and four months of calendar time, ending in a go/no-go decision with real evidence. Compare to committing $50–$100k to a full SEO program and discovering at month 9 that the niche wasn't rankable — the cheap test is where the real discipline lives.

## What to watch for in the 90-day window

- **Impressions trending up** is the earliest positive signal, even before positions improve. It means Google is showing your pages to searchers, which it does as it evaluates whether to rank them.
- **Positions moving from 40s → 20s → teens** over 60–90 days is a healthy trajectory. The last jump from position 11 to position 8 often takes much longer than the first jumps from 50 to 20, because page 1 is the hardest step.
- **Click-through rate** becomes meaningful only once positions are in the top 20. Below that, CTR is statistical noise.
- **Flat impressions with indexed pages** is the warning sign. It usually means Google doesn't consider the content relevant enough to surface — intent mismatch or depth gap. Sometimes it's an authority problem, but the flatness itself is the red flag.

## The honest alternative: validated-demand SEM

If your cheap test reveals strong SEM conversion on target queries but SEO rankings aren't moving by month 4–5, the honest read is often: **there's demand here, but your authority gap is too big for SEO to be the right channel this year.** The right move in that case is to run SEM on those queries now (validated, converting) while continuing a modest SEO program in the background as a year-2 asset — not to keep doubling SEO spend in hopes of cracking page 1 on a query you're structurally not ready for.

workedExample.setup: Jordan runs a $49/month SaaS for boutique hotel operations. His board has asked whether SEO is worth $8k/month for 12 months ($96k). He has $10k he can spend on a cheap test before committing. He has to make a recommendation in 4 months.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Jordan runs the cheap-test playbook.

**Week 1 — Keyword research.** He pulls 80 candidate queries across three clusters: boutique hotel operations ('boutique hotel front desk software,' 'small hotel PMS comparison'), specific workflows ('how to automate hotel housekeeping schedule,' 'hotel guest communication templates'), and adjacent vertical pain ('small hotel wifi system setup,' 'boutique hotel insurance requirements'). Combined monthly volume: ~42,000 searches across the long tail. Head terms like 'hotel PMS software' (22,000/mo) are dominated by Cloudbeds, Mews, and Little Hotelier — unrankable for 18+ months.

**Week 2 — SERP analysis.** Kills 20 queries where top 10 is entirely multi-thousand-RD sites. Keeps 60 where 3+ of the top 10 are thin, outdated, or low-authority — rankable within 12 months.

**Weeks 1–4 — SEM intent test ($1,200).** Runs Google Ads on 12 of the surviving queries with exact and phrase match, small daily budget. Results after 3 weeks:
- 7 queries convert at 2–5% (buyer intent confirmed).
- 3 queries convert near 0% (queries pull hobbyists and researchers, not buyers — killed from the SEO list even though their volumes looked good).
- 2 queries don't get enough clicks to conclude.

Final SEO candidate list: 45 queries (7 validated, plus 38 adjacent ones Jordan expects to behave similarly).

**Weeks 2–6 — Ship 4 test articles ($5,200).** He picks 4 queries spanning different difficulty levels:
- 'boutique hotel front desk software comparison' (1,200/mo, 6 top-10 competitors, medium difficulty).
- 'how to automate boutique hotel housekeeping' (400/mo, thin competition, easier).
- 'small hotel guest communication templates' (700/mo, some strong competitors, medium-hard).
- 'boutique hotel revenue per available room benchmark' (280/mo, thin competition, easier).

Each article is 2,500–3,500 words, with original data where possible, strong on-page, linked from 3 existing internal pages. He pitches 2–3 external links per article over the next 60 days and gets links on 2 of the 4 articles (1–2 links each).

**Weeks 6–16 — Monitor.** Search Console data:

- Week 6: all 4 articles indexed. Average positions 38, 51, 44, 33. Impressions growing slowly.
- Week 10: positions 22, 34, 28, 18. Impressions up ~4×. First trickle of clicks on the easiest article.
- Week 14: positions 14, 19, 15, 9. One article on page 1, others on page 2 climbing. Impressions up ~12× from week 6.
- Week 16: positions 11, 16, 12, 7. One article firmly on page 1; two others within striking distance of page 1.

**The decision at week 16.** Trajectory is healthy — 4 of 4 articles moved, one reached page 1, two are within 3–5 positions of page 1. Extrapolating: by month 9, he'd expect 3 of 4 on page 1 and meaningful organic traffic. Cheap test outcome: **go**. Recommendation to the board: scale to the $8k/month program, but keep SEM running on the validated-converting queries in parallel so near-term revenue continues while SEO matures.

**What a failed cheap test would have looked like.** If at week 16 all 4 articles were still in positions 30–50 with flat impressions, Jordan would have recommended against scaling — the signal would have said either the authority gap was larger than it looked, or the intent match was wrong, and $96k of additional content wouldn't have fixed either. The cheap test costs roughly 10% of the full program and prevents 90% of the wasted-content failures.

**Total cheap-test spend:** ~$7,000 over 16 weeks, producing a confident go/no-go decision before committing $96k. The key reframe: SEO cannot be A/B tested, but it can be *sampled* — a small batch of real articles and a patient 90-day watch reveal whether the full program is going to produce.

contrast.explanation: Bullseye's Step 4 (Test) is about running cheap, fast experiments to compare channels. The SEO-specific reality is that a traditional 2-week cheap test is impossible — the channel's slowest feedback loop is months, not weeks. This concept is what 'cheap testing' means when the slowest possible feedback loop is measured in 60–90 days: you cannot shorten the ranking timeline, but you *can* front-load every sub-question that has a faster answer (keyword demand, SEM intent validation, SERP feasibility) and then ship a small article batch whose 90-day trajectory decides the full program. The result is still a cheap test in Bullseye's sense — it spends ~10% of the full commitment to get a confident go/no-go — but the playbook looks different from a two-week SEM test.

flashcards.front: Why can't SEO be A/B tested the same way SEM can?
flashcards.back: Because the feedback loop is months, not weeks. SEM produces conversion data in days — you can settle a keyword's viability for $200 in two weeks. SEO rankings typically take 60–180 days to show meaningful movement, so a traditional short-cycle A/B test is impossible. 'Testing SEO' has to mean something different: front-loading every sub-question that has a faster answer, then sampling with a small batch of real articles and watching their 90-day trajectory.

flashcards.front: Name the four sub-questions a full SEO test decomposes into, and which ones can be answered quickly vs. slowly.
flashcards.back: (1) Does search demand exist? — answerable in an hour with keyword tools. (2) Do target queries convert commercially? — answerable in 2–4 weeks with a small SEM test. (3) Are the queries rankable given current authority? — answerable in a day via SERP analysis. (4) Can your specific content actually rank on the expected timeline? — answerable only over 60–90 days with published test articles. The first three are fast; only the fourth is irreducibly slow.

flashcards.front: The single most underused SEO pre-test is to run a small ______ campaign on your target keywords first. If paid clicks on those queries don't convert, organic rankings on those same queries ______ either — no matter how good the content.
flashcards.back: SEM (Google Ads) ; won't convert — this is the cleanest way to settle the commercial-intent sub-question months before SEO would reveal the answer on its own.

flashcards.front: At the 90-day check on 5 test SEO articles, which of these is the clearest healthy signal? (a) One article on page 1 with zero impressions on the others, (b) All 5 articles indexed with positions trending from 40s → 20s → teens and rising impressions, (c) All 5 articles in positions 3–10 with thousands of clicks, (d) All 5 articles indexed but in position 50+ with flat impressions.
flashcards.back: (b). Positions moving upward and impressions rising over 60–90 days is the standard healthy trajectory — articles reach page 1 incrementally, and the last jump from position 11 to page 1 often takes longer than the earlier jumps. (a) is inconsistent and likely flukey. (c) is unrealistically fast for 90 days. (d) is the clearest failure signal — stuck in mid-rankings with flat impressions usually means intent mismatch, depth gap, or authority shortfall.

flashcards.front: What does a flat-impressions-with-indexed-pages signal usually mean at the 90-day mark, and what's the diagnostic sequence?
flashcards.back: It usually means Google doesn't consider the content relevant enough to surface even on lower-ranked pages — most often an intent mismatch (the content format doesn't match what the query is asking for) or a content-depth gap (the article is thinner than ranking competitors). Diagnostic: (1) re-inspect the current top 10 for each query and check whether your content matches their format and depth; (2) check whether any pages have accidental noindex tags or canonical issues suppressing them; (3) check whether the authority gap is large enough that no new content would rank regardless. Flat impressions is the clearest early-warning signal of a structural mismatch.

scenarios.setup: A founder wants to commit $60k to a 12-month SEO program for a new B2C subscription product. He says: 'Let's just go — we can't know if it works until we do it.'
scenarios.question: Walk him through a cheap pre-commit test that could save most of the $60k if the channel isn't viable for his niche. What specific steps and what total spend?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The 'we can't know until we do it' framing is wrong — most of the uncertainty can be eliminated for ~10% of the full program cost. Specifically: (1) Week 1, spend 4 hours on keyword research in Google Keyword Planner / Ahrefs to identify 50–100 candidate long-tail queries with volume + competition data. Cost ~$100 in tools. (2) Week 2, spend a day inspecting SERPs for the top 20 candidate queries to estimate rankability (competitor referring-domain counts, content depth). Cost: just time. (3) Weeks 1–4, run a $600–$1,200 SEM test on 10 of the candidate queries to validate that clicks on these queries actually convert. Kill any query where SEM conversion is near zero. (4) Weeks 2–6, commission and publish 3–5 test articles ($3,000–$6,000 total) on the surviving high-priority queries, with full on-page and 2–3 external link pitches per article. (5) Weeks 6–16, monitor in Search Console: impressions, positions, indexing. At week 13–16, judge whether 3+ of the 5 articles show healthy upward trajectory. Total cheap-test spend: ~$4k–$8k and 4 months. Possible outcomes: (a) healthy trajectory → scale to the $60k program with confidence; (b) flat/stuck trajectory → don't scale, save $52–56k, redeploy to a different channel; (c) mixed trajectory → identify which query clusters worked and narrow the scaled program to those. The reframe: you can't A/B test SEO, but you can *sample* it — and sampling is 10% of the cost of full commitment. 'Just go' trades the test cost for a much larger program cost paid in full regardless of whether the channel works.
rubric: Rejects the 'just commit fully' framing
Lays out the four sub-questions or equivalent sequencing
Includes the SEM intent-validation step specifically
Quotes a realistic cheap-test cost (~10% of full commitment)
Names the three outcomes (go, no-go, narrow-the-scope)
Notes that sampling is different from A/B testing

scenarios.setup: A founder ran the cheap test: 4 test articles published, 90 days elapsed. Positions: 48, 51, 43, 50. Impressions: flat at very low numbers. All articles indexed. She says 'the trajectory hasn't started yet — let me wait another 3 months.'
scenarios.question: What's the likely diagnosis, and is waiting the right call?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The pattern — indexed but stuck in positions 40–50+ with flat impressions at 90 days — is one of the cleanest 'structural problem' signals SEO produces. Healthy trajectories show positions moving (from 40s to 20s to teens) and impressions rising, even if they haven't hit page 1 yet. Flat positions and flat impressions usually mean one of three things: (1) intent mismatch — the content format doesn't match what the query is asking for (e.g., a product pitch on a comparison query); (2) content depth gap — the articles are significantly thinner than the ranking competitors; (3) authority gap is too large — competitors have hundreds of referring domains and the new domain has near zero. Waiting another 3 months doesn't fix any of these. The right move: stop waiting and diagnose. (a) Reopen the top-10 SERP for each of the 4 queries and compare intent format and depth against the test articles. (b) Check authority profiles of ranking competitors. (c) Decide whether any of the 4 articles are salvageable with rewriting (intent or depth problem) or whether the queries themselves are structurally too hard (authority problem). If the diagnosis is authority gap, the honest conclusion may be that SEO isn't the right channel this year — redirect to SEM on the same validated-converting queries, and defer SEO to year 2 with a domain-authority-building program (PR, digital coverage, product-led link attraction) in the background. 'Let me wait another 3 months' without diagnosis is the classic way to convert a $5k test into a $25k test that produces the same answer.
rubric: Identifies the stuck-with-flat-impressions pattern as a structural failure signal
Names intent mismatch / depth gap / authority gap as the three likely causes
Rejects 'wait and see' without diagnosis
Prescribes concrete diagnostic steps (re-inspect SERPs, check authority)
Includes the 'defer SEO and run SEM instead' contingency

scenarios.setup: A founder says: 'Why do I need to run SEM on my target keywords first? That seems like extra spend. Can't I just publish the articles and see what happens?'
scenarios.question: Explain why the SEM pre-test is usually the most cost-effective single item in the cheap-test playbook.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Because SEM settles the commercial-intent question in 2–4 weeks for a few hundred dollars, where SEO would settle the same question in 6–12 months for thousands. If the query doesn't convert on SEM — where you're paying to put your site directly in front of searchers for that exact keyword — it won't convert on SEO either; the underlying user intent on the query is the same regardless of whether the click was paid or organic. The classic failure mode this catches: founders spend $10k on content targeting a query like 'free [product] template,' wait 9 months for it to rank, and then discover the query attracts only people looking for something free who never buy. A $500 SEM test would have revealed that in three weeks. The arithmetic: a $500–$1,000 SEM pre-test costs 1–2% of a year's SEO content budget and eliminates the most common specific SEO failure mode (good rankings on bad-intent queries). Skipping it to 'save the SEM spend' is the textbook false economy — you save $800 and then discover after 9 months that you spent $50k on articles that rank for queries that don't convert.
rubric: States that user intent is the same paid or organic
Names the specific failure mode (ranking for non-converting queries)
Gives the cost arithmetic (SEM test is 1–2% of a year's content budget)
Uses a concrete example of the kind of query this catches
Frames skipping the SEM test as false economy

teachback.prompt: Teach a founder how to cheaply test whether SEO will work for their business before committing to a full program. Cover: (1) why traditional A/B testing is impossible for SEO, (2) the four sub-questions and which can be answered fast, (3) the SEM pre-test trick and why it's the highest-leverage cheap test, (4) what a healthy 90-day trajectory looks like vs. a failure signal, (5) rough cost and calendar expectations.
rubric: Explains why SEO's slow feedback prevents traditional A/B testing
Names all four sub-questions with correct fast/slow categorization
Describes the SEM pre-test specifically and why it's so high-leverage
Describes a healthy trajectory (positions moving, impressions rising) and a failure signal (stuck positions, flat impressions)
States a realistic total cheap-test cost and timeline (~$5–10k, ~16 weeks)
Includes a go/no-go decision framework at the 90-day mark

pretest.prompt: A founder tells you: 'We spent 18 months on SEO. Traffic grew nicely for a while, then crashed 70% over a weekend and never came back.' Before reading on — what are the most likely causes of a sudden large SEO traffic drop, and which one is self-inflicted vs. externally imposed?
pretest.modelAnswer: Two dominant causes. (1) Google algorithm update — externally imposed. Major core updates happen several times a year, and sites that were over-optimized on tactics Google later deemed manipulative (or whose content was thin in retrospect, or whose links were paid/low-quality) can lose 50–80% of traffic in a single update. These are recoverable sometimes, but often the old ranking was on borrowed time. (2) Self-inflicted: black-hat tactics (bought links, PBNs, doorway pages) getting detected and penalized. Penalty drops are usually sharper and more permanent — they're Google explicitly telling you your techniques violated guidelines. The 'bought our way up and then crashed' trajectory is the textbook example. Other causes: site rebuild breaking technical SEO (robots.txt, redirects, canonicals); major internal change to content templates; or a single large competitor overhauling their content. A 70% overnight drop points strongly at algorithm update or penalty — the fix differs: algorithm updates are weathered by improving content quality and waiting; penalties require explicit cleanup and a reconsideration request.

lesson.markdown:
## Four canonical failure modes

Most SEO programs that fail, fail in predictable ways. Learning the patterns lets you diagnose your own program before it hits one of them.

## Pitfall 1 — Google algorithm changes

Google ships multiple algorithm updates a year, some of which — 'core updates' — materially rerank large sections of the web. Sites that rank on borrowed time (over-optimized anchor text, thin content padded to look deep, link profiles skewed toward low-quality sites) can lose 30–80% of traffic in a single update. Even well-made sites sometimes take collateral damage in updates aimed at something else.

This risk is irreducible but manageable. Two disciplines:

- **Diversify traffic sources.** A business where 80% of acquisition comes from Google organic is one algorithm update away from a near-extinction event. The mature pattern is SEO as one of several traction channels (SEM, content social, partnerships, direct), not the sole channel.
- **Build on fundamentals, not tactics.** Pages that rank because they're genuinely the best answer to a query rarely get hit by core updates aimed at low-quality content. Pages that rank because of specific tactical over-optimization (exact-match anchor text, thin doorway pages, keyword-stuffed paragraphs) are exactly what the updates target.

When a core update hits and you lose traffic, the diagnosis is usually not 'Google made a mistake.' It's 'Google recalibrated and the ranking you had was partly unearned.'

## Pitfall 2 — The slow-signal test-budget trap

The most common failure of a well-intentioned SEO program is running out of patience — or money — before the compounding starts. A team spends $15k/month for 5 months, sees minimal results, concludes 'SEO doesn't work for us,' and shuts it down. Three months later the articles that were sitting on page 2 would have moved to page 1 — except the team isn't there to see it.

The fix is budgetary, not tactical: if you cannot commit 9–12 months of SEO spend before the first serious evaluation, you cannot responsibly start an SEO program at all. The right move in that case is to delay SEO until funding or revenue extends your horizon enough to survive the lag. Half-funded SEO programs routinely convert 80% of their spend into pure waste because they quit right before compounding kicks in. Budget planning for SEO has to assume the cash flow for the full 12-month minimum, not the best-case 6-month rosy scenario.

## Pitfall 3 — Thin content farms

Before 2011, the standard playbook for long-tail SEO was to produce hundreds of 300–500 word articles on long-tail queries — low-quality but keyword-targeted. Google's Panda update (2011) and every major update since has specifically targeted this pattern. Thin content now not only fails to rank; it *drags down your entire domain's authority*, suppressing the pages that might otherwise have ranked.

The cheap content farm is a false economy. A domain with 1,000 thin articles ranking for nothing has lower rank potential for any *new* article than a domain with 100 great articles. Google treats the whole domain as a quality signal.

The modern AI version of this pitfall: generating thousands of AI articles cheaply and publishing them. Google's Helpful Content updates have specifically targeted this. Scale without depth is not a strategy; it's a penalty waiting to trigger.

## Pitfall 4 — Over-optimizing for metrics vs. users

The tactical checklists in SEO — keyword placement, internal linking, schema, anchor text — are easy to optimize in ways that harm user experience. Examples:

- Articles padded to 3,500 words because 'long content ranks' — when the user's actual question could be answered in 600 words.
- Internal link graphs engineered to pass authority rather than to help users navigate.
- Doorway pages — one page per keyword variation with 90% identical content, designed to capture search traffic rather than serve a reader.
- Aggressive exact-match anchor text on every inbound link.

Each of these can produce a short-term ranking lift and then get caught by an algorithm update. Worse, even when they're not caught, they produce user experiences bad enough that bounce rate rises and returning visits fall — which Google increasingly measures as ranking signals.

The reliable frame: **Google is trying to measure 'is this page genuinely useful for the query?'** Every shortcut that games a proxy for usefulness eventually gets detected, because Google's proxies keep getting better. The only durable strategy is to actually be useful.

## Pitfall 5 — Buying links

This is the one the original Traction chapter names most strongly, and it remains true: **don't buy links.** Every form of paid link-building — PBNs, paid guest posts, link exchange networks, expired-domain redirects, most 'high-DR backlink' services — is against Google's guidelines and gets caught eventually. The business model of link-selling depends on penalties being infrequent enough that sellers can stay in business; it does not depend on their customers surviving the penalties.

Black-hat link-building works in the short term. That's the temptation. Short-term ranking lifts from bought links fund the illusion that the tactic works. Then Google rolls out a detection update, the links get devalued or penalized, the site drops — and the recovery often requires disavowing the bought links (explicitly telling Google to ignore them) and then waiting months or years for the domain's reputation to recover. Companies have lost most of their organic traffic overnight and never recovered to prior levels.

The simplest discipline: any link source that involves paying the linking site itself is off-limits. Paying a writer to produce great content that organically earns links is fine. Paying a publisher to place a link is not.

## Putting it together: the failure taxonomy

- **Algorithm updates** — diversify traffic sources; build on fundamentals; weather the storms.
- **Slow-signal budget trap** — commit 12 months or don't start.
- **Thin content** — depth over volume, always; AI-generated filler is the modern version of the 2010 content farm.
- **Metric-over-user optimization** — Google measures usefulness increasingly directly; shortcuts don't compound.
- **Bought links** — hard no, even when they appear to work for a quarter.

The single unifying principle: SEO rewards durable quality and punishes shortcuts. Programs that survive 3+ years are programs that would produce good content even if Google didn't exist — the ranking is the byproduct, not the target.

workedExample.setup: Alex runs a mid-stage consumer finance startup. Their SEO program has produced strong results: 400 articles, 12 million annual organic visits, ~30% of signups. On a Monday morning, Search Console shows a 52% traffic drop starting Saturday. The team is panicking.
workedExample.walkthrough:
Alex walks the failure taxonomy in order, diagnosing what happened.

**Check 1 — Algorithm update?** He checks algorithm-tracking tools (Semrush Sensor, Moz MozCast) for volatility reports in the last 72 hours. Both show a major core update confirmed by Google on Friday. Likely cause candidate #1.

**Check 2 — Self-inflicted / penalty?** He checks Search Console's 'Manual Actions' tab — no manual penalty notices. That rules out an explicit penalty (but not algorithmic devaluation, which wouldn't show there).

**Check 3 — Technical / rebuild?** No deploys over the weekend. Search Console Coverage report unchanged. Pages still indexed. Rules out technical issue.

**Check 4 — Which pages were hit?** He compares impressions and clicks by URL pre- and post-update. Pattern: the drop is concentrated in two clusters — (a) AI-generated FAQ pages the team shipped 9 months ago to aggregate long-tail, and (b) a set of thin comparison pages templated to target hundreds of 'X vs Y' queries with 700–900 words each. His in-depth guides and original-research articles are mostly flat or slightly up.

**Diagnosis.** This is a Helpful Content-style core update that devalued thin and low-effort content at scale. Alex's cluster (a) — AI FAQs — is the textbook modern thin-content pitfall. Cluster (b) — templated comparison pages — is a gray-area bet that caught the update's backdraft. The team had shipped these as cheap long-tail coverage. The update removed the assumed ranking boost on those specific pages.

**Recovery plan.**
1. **Acknowledge reality.** The devalued rankings aren't coming back via tactical adjustments. The underlying content was thin; the update correctly devalued it. Restoration requires either deleting those pages or materially rewriting them.
2. **Kill cluster (a).** Delete the 120 AI-generated FAQ pages. They've been dragging down domain quality; removing them often produces a small overall site-wide lift because the domain's thin-content signal drops.
3. **Rewrite cluster (b).** Pick the top 40 'X vs Y' comparison pages by pre-update traffic. Invest $600 each rewriting them as real, detailed, differentiated comparisons with genuine research — 2,000–2,500 words each, not 700. Delete the remaining 150 that aren't worth the rewrite investment.
4. **Diversify.** Accelerate SEM spend and the email/newsletter program to reduce SEO share of total acquisition from 30% to something more like 15–20%. Not because SEO is dead, but because concentration was part of what made Saturday's update so painful.
5. **Commit to depth-only content going forward.** No more templated shortcut content. Every new article is long-form, researched, genuinely differentiated. Publication rate drops from 15/month to 5/month.

**Outcome 6 months later.** Domain-wide organic traffic has partly recovered (back to ~75% of peak). The in-depth content is ranking better than before, because the domain's overall quality signal improved after the thin content was removed. The team's SEO share of total acquisition is now 22% (down from 30%) with SEM and newsletter filling the gap. Cluster (a) is gone; cluster (b) is half what it was but ranking better per page. Alex's retrospective: 'We made two mistakes — building on thin content, and letting SEO become too high a share of acquisition. The update forced us to fix both. It was expensive, but the long-run business is more durable now than it was before the drop.'

**The lesson.** Most large SEO traffic drops are algorithmic reactions to tactics that were partly unearned in the first place. Recovery isn't about convincing Google to restore the old rankings; it's about fixing the underlying content quality and concentration risk that made the drop possible. Programs that walk the failure taxonomy proactively — before an update hits — don't need to walk it reactively.

contrast.explanation: The compounding-asset concept explains why SEO pays off *when it works* — durable content producing traffic for years at near-zero marginal cost. This concept explains what breaks the compounding: algorithm updates, budget collapse before payoff, thin content dragging down domain quality, metric-gaming that eventually gets caught, and paid links that crash the whole thing. The two concepts are mirror images: compounding is what SEO produces when you invest in durable quality and wait; the pitfalls are the ways you can convert that potential into waste. Understanding one without the other is dangerous — the compounding frame alone can make SEO look risk-free (which it isn't), and the pitfalls alone can make SEO look hopeless (which it isn't either). Durable quality + patience + channel diversification is the integrated posture.

flashcards.front: Name the five canonical SEO pitfalls.
flashcards.back: (1) Google algorithm updates — irreducible external risk; managed by channel diversification and building on fundamentals. (2) Slow-signal budget trap — running out of patience or money before compounding starts. (3) Thin content farms — producing volume without depth, which now drags down domain authority. (4) Over-optimizing for metrics vs. users — gaming proxies that Google's detection keeps catching up to. (5) Buying links — forbidden by Google's guidelines and eventually penalized, regardless of how well the short-term lift looks.

flashcards.front: Why is the 'slow-signal budget trap' the most common failure of well-intentioned SEO programs?
flashcards.back: Because SEO programs typically need 9–12 months before the compounding payoff starts, but teams routinely evaluate at month 4–6, see minimal results, and quit. The articles that were stuck on page 2 would have moved to page 1 a few months later — but the team isn't there to benefit. The fix is budgetary, not tactical: commit 12 months of spend or don't start. Half-funded SEO converts 80% of spend into waste because teams quit right before compounding kicks in.

flashcards.front: The single biggest SEO 'don't': do not ______ ______. It violates search engine guidelines, gets caught eventually, and the penalty recovery is often worse than never having had the short-term ranking boost.
flashcards.back: buy links — paying for link placements (PBNs, paid guest posts, link networks, 'high-DR backlink' services) is a textbook short-term lift followed by a ranking collapse when Google's detection catches up.

flashcards.front: A team is considering publishing 2,000 AI-generated articles targeting long-tail queries at $0.50 each. Will this strategy work?
flashcards.back: No. It's the modern version of the pre-2011 thin-content farm, and Google's Helpful Content updates have specifically targeted this pattern. Thin AI-generated content doesn't just fail to rank — it drags down the whole domain's authority, suppressing other pages that might otherwise have ranked. Scale without depth is not a strategy; it's a domain-quality penalty waiting to trigger. Depth beats volume, always.

flashcards.front: What's the unifying principle underneath all the SEO pitfalls?
flashcards.back: Google is trying to measure 'is this page genuinely useful for the query?' and its ability to measure this keeps improving. Every shortcut that games a proxy for usefulness eventually gets detected. The only durable strategy is to actually be useful. SEO rewards durable quality and punishes shortcuts; programs that survive 3+ years are programs that would have produced good content even if Google didn't exist — the ranking is the byproduct, not the target.

scenarios.setup: A founder is considering an SEO agency that promises 'page 1 rankings in 60 days, guaranteed, via our proprietary high-authority network.'
scenarios.question: What's the diagnosis, and what's your recommendation?
scenarios.modelAnswer: This is almost certainly a paid-link / PBN scheme. Three tells: (1) a 60-day guarantee on page 1 rankings is impossible for competitive queries through legitimate methods — the ranking timeline for earned content is 4–12 months minimum; (2) 'proprietary high-authority network' is the standard euphemism for a PBN or paid-placement service; (3) guarantees on rankings are by definition guarantees of manipulation, because legitimate SEO depends on Google's algorithm, which no agency can control. Recommendation: don't sign. The short-term pattern here is well-documented: page 1 rankings appear within the promised window, organic traffic spikes, the agency takes payment for 3–6 months, and then Google's detection catches up and the site drops — often below where it started, and often hard to recover. Recovery typically requires disavowing the paid links explicitly and waiting months to years for domain reputation to rebuild. The $3k/month the agency costs is better spent on (a) a legitimate content-production team that produces 3–5 genuinely strong articles per month, (b) a PR program targeting industry publications for natural coverage and earned links, (c) product investments that make parts of the product naturally link-attracting (free tools, public pages, shareable outputs). Slower, compounding, legal.
rubric: Identifies the offer as almost certainly paid links / PBN
Names the three tells (60-day guarantee, 'proprietary network' euphemism, impossibility of guaranteed rankings)
Describes the short-term-lift-then-crash pattern
Redirects to specific legitimate alternatives
Notes the difficulty and length of penalty recovery

scenarios.setup: A startup's SEO was their primary channel — 70% of signups came from organic search. A Google core update reduced organic traffic by 55% overnight, and it hasn't recovered over 3 months. The founder asks whether they should double-down on SEO to recover, diversify away from it, or both.
scenarios.question: Walk through your recommendation. What's the short-term vs. long-term posture?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The 70% concentration was itself a strategic risk, and the update has made that visible. The honest posture is both — but in a specific order and with a different shape than the pre-update program had. Short-term (next 90 days): (a) Diagnose which pages were hit and why. If the pattern is thin content or over-optimized pages (exact-match anchor clusters, doorway-style pages, templated long-tail without real depth), accept the diagnosis and kill or rewrite those pages. Don't try to restore the old rankings; they were partly unearned. (b) Aggressively spin up alternative channels — SEM on validated-converting queries, email/newsletter programs, paid social if the audience fits, PR for earned links, partnerships. The immediate goal is to reduce SEO share of acquisition from 70% to 30–40% within 90 days, not by cutting SEO but by growing the others. (c) Don't panic-cut the SEO team; the compounding asset still produces for in-depth articles that weren't hit. Medium-term (3–12 months): (d) Rebuild the SEO program with depth, not volume. Fewer, much better articles. Kill every thin page that's dragging domain quality. Invest in PR and real link-earning rather than tactical link-building. (e) Expect partial recovery but not a return to the pre-update peak — the old peak was partly inflated by tactics Google has now devalued. Long-term (12+ months): (f) Treat the update as a correction that revealed a diversification problem, not as an attack. A mature business has multiple sources of acquisition; the founder's real lesson is that no channel should be 70% of the mix regardless of how well it's performing.
rubric: Identifies the 70% concentration as a pre-existing strategic risk
Prescribes honest diagnosis before recovery efforts
Names diversification as the short-term priority (not SEO restoration)
Prescribes depth-over-volume for the rebuilt SEO program
Manages expectations: partial recovery, not full restoration
Frames the update as a correction revealing concentration risk

scenarios.setup: A founder has 7 months of runway and is considering starting a new SEO program with $6k/month ($42k total).
scenarios.question: Walk through the budget-trap analysis. Should she start the program?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Probably not — at least not as a primary channel investment. The slow-signal budget trap is the specific failure mode at play: SEO typically needs 9–12 months before compounding payoff starts, and a 7-month horizon cannot survive that lag. $42k spent over 7 months with near-zero returns for the first 5–6 months means that if the business doesn't raise more cash or generate significant other revenue by month 6, she runs out before the articles rank. The articles that would have moved to page 1 in month 9 never get there, and she's converted $42k into almost pure waste. Three better options depending on specifics: (1) Delay SEO entirely. Spend the $42k on faster-signal channels (SEM, sales, partnerships) that produce customers inside her runway, extending runway via revenue. Start SEO later, when the horizon is longer. (2) Scale SEO way down — $1–2k/month as a small bet that might pay off later, with the rest going to fast channels. Accept that the SEO program is more of a seed than a commitment. (3) Raise a bridge round specifically earmarked for the SEO lag, so the horizon matches the asset's maturation timeline. This can make sense if the founder has high conviction about SEO and investors willing to underwrite the specific bet. The cleanest rule: if you cannot commit 12 months of SEO spend before the first serious evaluation, you cannot responsibly start an SEO program at the primary-channel level. 7 months doesn't clear that bar.
rubric: Identifies the 7-month runway as insufficient for SEO's 9–12 month lag
Names the specific failure mode (budget trap)
Prescribes at least two of: delay SEO, scale down to seed level, raise a bridge
States the 12-month commitment rule
Doesn't conclude 'never do SEO' — just 'not now at this scale'

teachback.prompt: Teach a founder the five canonical ways SEO programs fail, so they can recognize and avoid each. Cover: (1) algorithm updates and why diversification matters, (2) the slow-signal budget trap and the 12-month rule, (3) thin content farms (including the modern AI version), (4) metric-over-user optimization, (5) buying links. For each pitfall, give a concrete symptom a founder would see and the specific discipline that prevents it.
rubric: Names all five pitfalls with accurate descriptions
Gives a concrete symptom for each
Names the specific preventive discipline for each
Mentions channel diversification for algorithm-update risk
States the 12-month commitment rule for the budget trap
Identifies the AI-content version of thin content farms as the modern variant
States the unifying principle (Google measures usefulness increasingly directly; shortcuts eventually fail)
